raw_text:
J.P. Sullivan, 'Philology and Literary Criticism at the Graduate Level', Arethusa
2 (1969) 1-12.
104.
Raoul Verdière, 'La Bucolique post-virgilienne', Eos 56 (1966) 161-185.
105.
К. Vretska, 'Gedanken uber den jungen Vergil', Gestalt u. Wirklichkeit Festgabe f. Ferd. Weinhand
(Berlin, 1967), 337-47.
106.
J.H. Whitfield, 'Virgil into Dante', op. cit. (14) 94-118.
107.
R.D. Williams, 'Changing Attitudes to Virgil', op. cit. (14) 119-138.
108.
Gordon Williams.
Tradition and Originality in Roman Poetry, Oxford, 1968.
Armstrong's article (61) provides a careful assessment of recent interpretations
of the epic. Michael Ayrton, the modern artist and sculptor (62), finds the
sources of his inspiration for repeated versions of Daedalus, Icarus, Sibyl, and
Minotaur in the combined influence of Berlioz, the numinous quality of the acropolis of Cumae, and the encouragement of Jackson Knight. Betts (63) juxtaposes
lines, images, and ideas used in common or in similar fashion by Shakespeare and
Vergil. Chapman's translation (65), with notes, is catastrophic. Christern (66)
examines the temple on the upper terrace of the Cumaean acropolis, originally
Greek, plundered (no doubt) and rebuilt by Augustus, ultimately transformed into
a Christian basilica. Collinge's remarks on ambiguity in literature (67) merit close
reading by critics of Vergil. Dale (68) comments on trochaic caesura in the third
foot, especially in Aeneid 6, 322-5, where it occurs in four consecutive lines. Doig
(69) explores proper names and local epithets with Greek roots or associations and
suggests that Vergil, with a proven interest in etymological associations, had wider
possibilities in mind when he selected Greek words or words with Greek associations.
Duckworth's article (70) on hexameter poetry puts Vergil into larger context. The
same scholar's study of the first foot dactyl comprising three words (71) suggests
that the higher frequency in the Georgics as opposed to the Aeneid may indicate
Lucretian influence on the didactic poems, or as a corollary, a desire to find
greater dignity and majesty for the epic by the reduction in the usage. Duckworth
(72) also shows, in a metrical study of Maphaeus Vegius' Thirteenth Book of the
Aeneid, published in 1428, that the Renaissance metric is basically dissimilar to
that of Vergil. D.C. Earl (73) treats 'nobilitas', 'virtus'
and 'gloria' as key words
and concepts in both Vergil and Horace. For Turnus, 'virtus' is linked with 'gloria',
and it is only in dealing with Turnus that the aristocratic concept of 'nobilitas' is
mentioned; Earl's remarks would suggest that Turnus is the solitary Roman 'nobilis'
in the epic. Sallust had alleged already that a man's 'virtutes' not his family-tree,
should constitute the passport to 'gloria'. Turnus 'virtus' belongs to remoter peoples,
rustic Italians, and early Romans, but his thirst for fame ('gloria') is reprehensible
and as foreign to 'virtus' as is 'nobilitas'. Turnus is seemingly the embodiment of
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:17 UTC
51
the 'nobiles', whose factional struggles for private ends and for the grand 'figura7
impaired and finally destroyed the Republic. Earl's study of the Augustan Age (74)
provides valuable asides on Vergil and the literary scene. Edinger's article (75) is
concerned with assonance and its quantity in the Eclogues concentrating on the
single syllable 'or'. Felperin (76) provides a verse translation of Aeneid 2, 506-558.
Gossage (80) shows that Statius and Silius Italicus were much closer to Vergil than
Valerius Flaccus. Silius' produced an anachronistic imitation; Statius* Thebaid is
closest to the Vergilian spirit and poetic technique.
Guadagno (81) continues the discussion of the C. Cornelius Gallus inscription on the Vatican obelisk with reference to the poet's tragic death which had such
dolorous implications for Vergil. Haarhoff (82) argues that in the material world
Vergil supported Augustan policy from inner conviction as the best available. But
Vergil was no instrument of Augustan propaganda for he looks down on him from
a great spiritual height. Hagendahl's monumental study (84) reveals the marked
influence of Cicero and of Vergil on Augustine, and of Sallust and Terence in
lesser degree. Howell's article (87) on the meanings of postis explains Aeneid 2,
480 and 493: postes refers to the leaves which are torn off the pivot-pole (cardo)
not just the sockets. Vergil also refers to one of the disadvantages of the Roman
type of door (Aeneid 1, 449):
the pivot-pole became stiff and groaned in its sockets.
McKay's address (89) treats reflections of architecture and the fine arts in the Aeneid
from the standpoint of technique and subject matter. Vergil's epic as pictorial composition is better appreciated when viewed in the tradition of Roman (and Hellenistic) art, historical, allegorical, and triumphal. Vergil has frequently been acknowledged as an expert among landscape painters who have used words instead of pigments.
McKay's chapter (90) in the Dudley collection shows how Poussin, Claude, and Turner
dealt with Vergilian landscape, each responsive and faithful in his peculiar fashion. The
painters often revealed a sureness of taste and judgment that overshadows the literary
critics of their time and our own. Otis' article (92) on the unique quality of Roman
literature includes Vergil in its purview. Paget's articles (93, 94) describe the Greek and
Roman ports of Cumae, particularly the military harbour of Agrippa beneath the acropolis on the Tyrrhenian shoreline where the menace of Sextus Pompey was omnipresent
after 40 B.C. Paget's naval experience served him well in locating the two lighthouses
along the shore, traces of moles, and the line of a Roman canal from nearby Lake
Fusaro (the ancient Acherusian Swamp) that supplied scouring water for the Roman
port beneath the Grotto of the Sibyl. He has also determined the original design of
the Portus Julius (Georgics 2, 161ff). Pollard's lecture (96) examines Vergil's ambivalent attitude in his most profound passages (Aeneid 1, 461; 8, 65; Georgics 1, 169f.
Aeneid 3, 381f., Aeneid 6, 28f., etc. Savage (98) analyses Georgics 3, 563-66, and 4,
559-66, and suggests that Vergil's epitaph (Mantua me genuitf etc.) follows a pattern
which may allude conceptually to the favorite maxim of Augustus - 'festina lente'
(Suet. Aug. 25, 4). Segal (100) and Sullivan (103) both provide valuable observations
and counsel in matters of modern literary criticism. Silhanek's prose version of the
Aeneid (101) is heavily abridged and paired with an abridged Iliad for secondary school
students. Sullivan (102) tries to show that suffering in Vergil always has a meaning,
partly discernible in this life, wholly revealed in the after-life. Vergil sensed that great
achievements are coupled with great suffering, that nobility of character needs testing
in the fires of affliction, and that love inevitably involves sacrifice. Whitfield (106)
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:17 UTC
52
argues that Vergil and Dante are in most things quite dissimilar both in statement and
in poetic tone. Williams (107) explores attitudes to Vergil from Dryden to Tennyson,
from the hard robust approach to the near sentimentality of the Victorians. Gordon
Williams' egregious volume (108) treats, inter alia, Vergil's conception of the underworld, dislocations of word-order, the handling of political issues, linguistic inventions,
mock-heroir treatment of animals, and the poet's power of literary visualization and
expertise with similes.
109.
Sir Roger Mynors, Vergil
Oxford, 1969.
The new Oxford University Classical Text replaces Sir Arthur Hirtzel's edition
with a revised text and the addition of an index nominum. The new apparatus
criticus provides a fuller account of the indirect tradition and incorporates the
evidence of ninth-century manuscripts.
110.
W.A. Camps. An Introduction to Virgil's A eneid.
Oxford, 1969. The discussion
of particular topics is designed to convey an overall impression of the epic. The
book is available as a paperback and is highly recommended for study at all levels.
A. G. McKay
McMaster University
September 1, 1969
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:17 UTC
